transcript: A woman envies the beautiful and fashionable women at her workplace, longing to be a part of their circle. Seeing a TV ad for a beauty lotion that promises a radical transformation, she can't help but try it out. Unfortunately, the product brings out the worst in her. In a suburban home during the Christmas season, a woman eats dinner in front of the TV. Then, hearing a noise in the basement, she grabs an axe and goes down to investigate. She talks to a large, stuffed bear surrounded by numerous other preserved animals, asking if it has seen anyone. She hears another noise from upstairs, so she goes up to check the bedroom and the bathroom, finding nothing. The woman, Stacy, calls the Glen Ellyn Police Department and reports the strange noises to Officer Keith Chapman, her husband. Keith assures her that the old house makes such noises in a routine manner, suggesting he has entertained her calls plenty of times before. However, before Stacey can finish her speculation that someone's in the house, Keith ends the call. The next day, at the bank where Stacey works, she listens to her female colleagues gossiping. The women talk at lengths about physical appearances, and Stacey gets mesmerized by one of them, Gina, rubbing lotion on her hands. That night, Stacey is surprised to find out from her co-worker that she's invited to Gina's secret Santa party. She then timidly draws the last lot containing the name Gina. Stacy spends her free time shooting down a duck, which she skins and stuffs afterward, much like the rest of her taxidermy collection. Then, Keith gets home and Stacy excitedly tells him about being invited to Gina's party. She feels relieved at the chance to finally fit in with her co-workers, envying Gina's attractive features and friendly nature. So, Keith reminds Stacey she did make friends with him, and goes to have dinner in the living room while his wife finishes up the stuffed duck. The following night, Stacey arrives at Gina's impressive-looking house carrying a large present. Inside, the women gossip at length about their escapades and beauty regiments. Stacey observes quietly, an ugly figurative duck orbiting a circle of glamorously dressed women. Concluding the chatter with $25,000 facelifts, Gina proceeds to give out gifts to the guests, including a thoroughly confused Stacey who thought it was a secret Santa party. They all receive a set of expensive, aloe-glow products, much to the delight of everyone. Stacey then hands her large present to Gina, who opens it with excitement fueled by the chanting guests. Inside is the stuffed duck, which Stacey explains how she shot, skinned, stuffed, and sewed the animal back in grisly detail. The women barely contain their revulsion as Gina puts the duck back in its box, to Stacey's dismay. Gina turns the mood around by highlighting the aloe glow gifts, prompting the guests to test out the lotion on themselves. Stacey watches her colleagues all enjoying the feel of the product on their skin, trying it out herself. While the women revel in the lotion's soothing sensation, Stacey, who applied it to her face, notes that it feels warm. One woman points out Stacey's reddening face, causing the others to recoil in disgust. She soon leaves the party well before everyone else, as Gina tells her to keep the ice pack on her face. Stacey apologizes for spoiling the night, but Gina waves it off and urges her to go home almost impatiently. Before the words Merry Christmas completely leave Stacey's mouth, she turns around to find the door already shut. During dinner at home, Stacey grumbles to Keith about being allergic to the best lotion in the world. Keith figures she has sensitive skin, recalling a perfume similar effect on her in the past, which does nothing to console Stacey. She blames herself and says she has a bad face, letting slip what seems to be a lifelong struggle with herself. Unable to sleep that night, Stacey stares in the bathroom mirror, examining the rashes on her face with resentful eyes. Meanwhile, in the living room, the Aloe Glow lotion oozes out of its tube like an organism, just as Stacey turns on the TV and sits on the couch. She flips through channels until an Aloe Glow ad catches her attention. In it, a white-haired man promotes the beautifying benefits of Aloe Glow, including a strangely specific message for Stacey. As Stacey shifts uncomfortably on the couch, the Aloe Glow man straight up addresses her through the TV, urging her to call the number on the screen and change her ugly duckling situation. The man's attractive female assistant then lists a number of side effects that range from stiff joints all the way to a lack of individualization. Initially wide-eyed with shock, Stacy starts laughing at the ridiculous possibility of talking to the TV. However, the white-haired man promises that Aloe Glow can fix her and give her peace of mind if only she would use the Aloe Glow lotion lying on her table. Stacy rebuts that the lotion gave her a rash rash, but the aloe glow man counters that she needs to use the entire tube and more for a full transformation. Stacey also complains about the product making her itch, but the man insists it's meant to do that, claiming that her skin is itching because it's healing. The man goads Stacey to pick up the phone once more, highlighting her insecurity while spewing some twisted flattery. All throughout this spiel, Stacey gradually internalizes the man's words, pushing her to call the Aloe Glow number. After Stacey places her order, the Aloe Glow man leaves one last message to use the lotion, and she picks up the tube from the table. The next morning, a large Aloe Glow box is delivered to Stacey's front door. She takes the box down to the basement, cuts it open, and picks up a tube. Later, Keith sees Stacey watching TV while scratching her face and neck, noting that her rashes seem to have gotten worse. He suggests stop using the lotion, but Stacey dismisses the itching as part of the healing process, parroting the Aloe Glow ad. Keith states how companies like Aloe Glow profit off women's insecurities and that he finds nothing wrong with Stacey. Stacey contends that there is something wrong with her and that Keith wouldn't understand because people don't hold men to the same standards as women. She appreciates her husband's kind words, but insists she needs him to be supportive. Keith then gives in and convinces her to stop scratching, but she gets back at it once he leaves. Throughout the day, Stacey continues to apply copious amounts of aloe glow on her body. Glued to the TV come nighttime, she comes across the aloe glow ad again, later checking to see if her skin is making any progress besides the persistent itching. That night, Keith wakes up to the sound of Stacey scratching her worsening rashes and tells her to stop. Stacey reiterates the Aloe Glow ad's claim that itch equals healing, but Keith makes her put on oven mitts. While Stacey retrieves the mitts downstairs, the TV turns on by itself and the Aloe Glow man pops into view. He beckons a wary Stacey to the living room to observe her progress. Stacey counters that her rashes are getting worse due to the allergy, but the man rebuffs the notion saying the lotion is doing its job. He insists that she needs to let the new Stacey grow but feels that she doesn't trust him. The TV then goes blank, as if the Aloe Glow Man takes offense at Stacey's misgivings. Stacey pleads with the Aloe Glow Man to come back and he does, this time spurring her to go all the way. Stacey voices her fear of the resulting pain, but the man asks her if she would rather feel the pain of exclusion. He further leans on Stacey's desire to be pretty, weaving promises of a new her just waiting under her skin. The man bids her to use more aloe-glow before the TV turns off. When Stacey goes to the basement to pick up another aloe-glow, the tubes pop their caps and squeeze their contents out by themselves. Spooked, Stacy dashes back to bed and tucks herself in with Keith, asking him if he can stay home the next day, but Keith says he can't. Meanwhile, the aloe glow products continue to seep out of their tubes, filling the box. The night goes on, and the lotion eventually spills onto the floor as a shape seems to emerge from the gooey puddle. The following day, Stacy discovers her rashes have become crusty but is somehow pleased with it. Keith arrives home to eat a meal, and Stacey shows him the results of her lotion regimen. Contrary to her excitement, Keith is troubled by Stacey's skin looking like it's about to peel off and recommends they visit a doctor. Undeterred, Stacey echoes Aloe Glow's spiels and selling points, completely buying into the dream the white-haired man's holder. When Keith tries reassuring her that she's lovely inside and out, Stacey takes offense and exclaims that she wants to throw away her skin. She then storms downstairs as Keith warns her to stop using the lotion. In the basement, Stacey finds that the aloe glow lotion has assumed a faceless female shape, tall and slender amid the taxidermy shelves. At first, it only mirrors her movements, but then she holds hands with it, eventually kissing and tenderly embracing the lotion being. Afterward, Stacey grabs a scalpel on her way to the living room, where Keith is aghast to see his wife slathered with lotion. She expresses excitement, but her husband reiterates that it's not her, citing her passion for horror movies, nature, and taxidermy. Keith underlines how Stacey can create something beautiful from what other people simply see as a dead animal. Stacey rebuffs his words, her unfocused eyes glinting madly as she vaguely proclaims the arrival of her new self, causing Keith to angrily refute her transformation. Brandishing the scalpel, Stacey confirms that neither of them will budge from their points of view and stabs Keith in the head. Dumbfounded at his wife's actions, Keith sits down with his scalpel stinking out of his forehead, debating whether he should pull it out. While Stacey looks on apologetically, Keith pulls out the blade as blood gushes down his face, asking his wife for a towel while he rages for help. Before Keith can tell the police dispatcher his condition, Stacey swings an axe on his back. Confused and dizzy from the blood loss, Keith falls to the floor as Stacey continues to hack him to death. She then goes upstairs, following a set of lotion footprints to the bathroom where the humanoid aloe glow stands poised over the bathtub. It leans over as if doing a swan dive, pouring itself inside until the tub is filled with lotion. Stacy takes off her clothes and submerges her flaky, rash-covered body into the lotion bath. Stacy enjoys the sensation of the aloe glow seemingly throbbing throughout her body, like something alive is consuming her. Later that night, Stacey emerges from the bathroom coated in a thick layer of lotion, giddily strutting through the house. Shedding the lotion bit by bit, Stacey looks in the mirror and finds that her skin is now smooth, her eyes are aligned, and her teeth are straight. She rushes downstairs to showcase her transformation to Keith's corpse, momentarily forgetting her heinous act in her excitement. Then, she pushes her husband's body down the basement and later disembowels him. The next day, Stacey cuts her hair, puts on makeup and accessories, and completes her chic look with a pair of oversized sunglasses. She bids a stuffed Keith propped up on the couch goodbye and disposes of his organs outside before leaving for work. Stacey arrives at the bank, the guard praising her appearance as her colleagues stare in disbelief. She walks confidently to her desk while the other women trail her in fascination. Gina and the others chat with Stacey, admiring her new look and finally giving her the validation she's longed for. Then, Stacey starts gossiping with them, reveling in shallow attention and adoration.
